era_context: The late 12th century CE was a period of significant geopolitical realignment and cultural development. In the Levant, the Crusader states were facing increasing pressure from Saladin's rising Ayyubid power. In Western Europe, the Angevin Empire under Henry II was experiencing internal tensions that would lead to the Great Rebellion. In East Asia, the Jin Dynasty controlled the north and the Song Dynasty the south, both experiencing cultural and economic development. The Islamic world was undergoing consolidation under figures like Saladin.

events.title: The Great Rebellion Against Henry II
events.description: In 1173, the sons of Henry II of England—Richard, Henry the Young King, and Geoffrey—rebelled against their father, encouraged by their mother Eleanor of Aquitaine and supported by King Louis VII of France. The rebellion was motivated by disputes over inheritance, the division of territories, and Henry II's refusal to grant his sons real power. The Great Rebellion lasted until 1174 and resulted in Henry II's military victory, but it revealed the fragility of the Angevin Empire and foreshadowed its eventual collapse.
key_figures: Eleanor of Aquitaine (Queen, instigator of rebellion)
Richard (future Richard I, Count of Aquitaine)
Louis VII (King of France, supporter of rebellion)
events.certainty_note: The Great Rebellion of 1173–1174 is extensively documented in English, Norman, and French sources. The date of its outbreak in 1173 is firmly established.
cross_references: 1189-001 (Henry II's death, consequence of tensions revealed by rebellion)

events.title: Saladin's Consolidation of Ayyubid Power in Egypt
events.region: North Africa and Levant (Egypt and Syria)
events.description: Saladin continued his consolidation of power in Egypt and the Levant in 1173. He was systematically eliminating rival factions, including remnants of the Fatimid dynasty and competing Zengid lords. His administrative reforms were centralizing authority and creating a more efficient military and bureaucratic system. By 1173, Saladin had established himself as the dominant Islamic power in the eastern Mediterranean.

sources.name: Baha ad-Din Ibn Shaddad, Life of Saladin
Imad ad-Din al-Isfahani, writings on Saladin's rise

events.certainty_note: Saladin's consolidation of power in the 1170s is well-documented, but specific events and dates within 1173 are less precisely recorded. Dating is approximate within ±1–2 years.

events.title: Jin Dynasty Stability Under Emperor Shizong
events.description: Emperor Shizong of the Jin Dynasty maintained stable rule over northern China in 1173. His reign was marked by administrative competence, cultural patronage, and the consolidation of Jurchen rule over Han Chinese territories. The Jin court at Zhongdu was a center of cultural synthesis and learning.

sources.name: Jin Shi (History of the Jin Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: Jin Dynasty chronology is well-established. Emperor Shizong's reign is firmly dated to 1161–1189.

events.title: Southern Song Dynasty Prosperity and Innovation
events.description: The Southern Song Dynasty continued its economic expansion and technological innovation in 1173. Maritime trade was flourishing, with Song merchants dominating Indian Ocean commerce. Urban centers were growing, and innovations in shipbuilding, navigation, printing, and ceramics were advancing. The Song economy was the most sophisticated in the world at this time, with sophisticated financial instruments and commercial networks.

sources.name: Song Shi (History of the Song Dynasty)
Contemporary Song economic and trade records
Archaeological evidence from Song ports and sites

events.certainty_note: Song Dynasty economic prosperity in this period is well-documented in official histories and surviving economic records.

events.title: Crusader Kingdom of Jerusalem Under Pressure
events.description: The Crusader Kingdom of Jerusalem faced increasing military pressure from Saladin's expanding Ayyubid forces in 1173. The kingdom was experiencing internal divisions and economic strain. King Amalric I had died in 1174, and his young son Baldwin IV (the Leper King) was struggling to maintain control. The kingdom's military resources were stretched thin, and its position was becoming increasingly precarious.
key_figures: Baldwin IV (young heir, the Leper King)
events.certainty_note: The general situation of the Crusader Kingdom in the 1170s is well-documented, but specific events and dates within 1173 are less precisely recorded. Dating is approximate within ±1–2 years.
cross_references: 1176-001 (Battle of Hattin, culmination of Crusader decline)

disconfirming_evidence: The Great Rebellion is sometimes portrayed as a simple family conflict driven by Eleanor of Aquitaine's ambitions, but modern scholarship emphasizes the structural tensions within the Angevin Empire. The rebellion was not merely a personal dispute but reflected fundamental conflicts over succession, the distribution of power, and the nature of feudal loyalty. Additionally, the characterization of Henry II as a strong ruler who easily suppressed the rebellion obscures the fact that the rebellion revealed the fragility of his authority and the deep resentment of his sons. The rebellion's aftermath—Eleanor's imprisonment and the continued tensions between Henry and his sons—would eventually lead to the empire's fragmentation after Henry's death in 1189.
historiographic_note: The year 1173 CE is well-documented in Western European, Islamic, and East Asian sources. European sources provide detailed accounts of the Great Rebellion; Islamic sources document Saladin's consolidation of power; Chinese sources record the Jin and Song dynasties. However, the historical record is heavily weighted toward political and military events. Economic, social, and demographic changes are less visible except in East Asia, where Song economic records are relatively abundant. Sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia (beyond the Ghurid expansion), the Americas, and Oceania are largely absent from surviving written records.

graph_edges.note: The Great Rebellion of 1173 was suppressed by Henry II in 1174
The tensions revealed by the Great Rebellion contributed to the eventual fragmentation of the Angevin Empire after Henry II's death
Saladin's consolidation in 1173 was accelerated by Nur ad-Din's death in 1174
The Crusader Kingdom's weakness in 1173 culminated in the catastrophic Battle of Hattin in 1176
